questions.questionText: A patient falls on an outstretched hand (FOOSH). X-ray shows a transverse fracture of the distal radius with dorsal angulation and displacement ('dinner fork' deformity). Diagnosis?
questions.optionB: Colles fracture (most common wrist fracture — distal radius fracture with dorsal displacement/angulation, common in osteoporotic elderly women)
questions.explanation: Core concept: Colles = distal radius, dorsal displacement, dinner fork deformity. Why correct: FOOSH mechanism in elderly (osteoporotic bone). Extra-articular distal radius fracture. Dorsal displacement, dorsal angulation, radial deviation, impaction, supination. Dinner fork deformity (lateral view). Why A wrong: Smith fracture = REVERSE Colles — volar/palmar displacement ('garden spade' deformity). Mechanism: fall on back of hand or direct blow. Why C wrong: Barton = intra-articular distal radius fracture-dislocation (dorsal or volar lip). Why D wrong: Galeazzi = distal radius shaft fracture + DRUJ dislocation (isolated radius = Galeazzi). Clinical relevance: Complications: malunion, median nerve compression (acute CTS), EPL tendon rupture, CRPS (Sudeck's atrophy). Exam trap: Colles = Dorsal (D for Dorsal); Smith = Volar (reverse of Colles). Most common fracture overall in elderly women. Memory aid: 'Colles = Coffee spill (FOOSH) = Dorsal displacement = Dinner fork.' Reference: Maheshwari – Upper Limb Fractures.
questions.conceptTag: Colles fracture dorsal displacement dinner fork FOOSH

questions.questionText: In a supracondylar fracture of the humerus in a child, the most commonly injured nerve is:
questions.optionB: Anterior interosseous nerve (AIN — branch of median nerve; tested by asking child to make 'OK' sign — inability = AIN palsy; also radial/median nerve can be injured)
questions.explanation: Core concept: Supracondylar fracture — most common elbow fracture in children (extension type 97%). Why correct: Extension type: AIN (branch of median nerve) most commonly injured nerve. Tests: FPL (thumb IP flexion) + FDP to index finger (DIP flexion) — 'OK' sign inability. Brachial artery most commonly injured vessel. Why A wrong: Ulnar nerve — injured in medial epicondyle fractures and flexion-type supracondylar fractures. Why C wrong: Musculocutaneous — shoulder injuries. Why D wrong: Axillary nerve — shoulder dislocation, proximal humerus fractures. Clinical relevance: Gartland classification (I-III). Volkmann's ischemic contracture: brachial artery damage → forearm compartment syndrome → contractures if missed. 5 P's of compartment syndrome. Exam trap: Extension type = AIN (median branch); Flexion type = ulnar nerve. Know which nerve for which type. Memory aid: 'Supracondylar Extension = AIN Extending out = OK sign lost.' Reference: Maheshwari – Pediatric Fractures.
questions.conceptTag: Supracondylar fracture AIN median nerve Volkmann
